retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: The healthcare provider must conduct a thorough interview and document the patient's medical history. If the lesions appear restricted to teeth, jaw, or other dental hard tissues, intraoral or panoramic radiography should be done to obtain the necessary information for an accurate diagnosis. If the knowledge gained from the radiography is insufficient to diagnose the problem and the patient does not require urgent irreversible surgery like tooth extraction, palliative treatment should be given while monitoring the patient's condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: CBCT imaging must only be indicated in specific cases where the operator has a clinical question that panoramic or intraoral radiographs cannot answer. [17] Up-to-date data shows that using CBCT before removing mandibular third molars does not reduce nerve injury or lead to better patient outcomes than panoramic radiographs. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative CBCT before tooth extraction is mainly related to impacted mandibular third molars due to the risk of injuring the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN) during surgery. [8] However, the radiograph of choice before removing mandibular third molars is orthopantomography. This x-ray will provide information about the proximity of the tooth with the IAN. The most common radiographic signs associated with IAN injury are the darkening of the roots of the third molar, interruption of the cortical lines of the canal, and diversion of the canal. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor and characteristics are assessed using the patient’s history, clinical exam, and imaging, most commonly computed tomography (CT). On CT scan, ameloblastomas appear as radiolucent (radiographically dark) lesions of the maxilla or mandible, often with multilocular or “soap-bubble” appearance, bony expansion and tooth root resorption. They may also be associated with unerupted teeth. A definitive diagnosis of ameloblastoma is with a surgical biopsy, which shows characteristic histologic findings. It is essential to differentiate the entity from a primary intraosseous squamous cell carcinoma. The World Health Organization (WHO) classified malignant ameloblastoma into two types [3] :

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Structure and Function -- Zygomatic Process
retrieved_docs.content: The zygomatic process projects laterally from the maxilla, forming the superolateral border of the maxillary sinus. This structure lies above the 1st maxillary molar and articulates inferiorly with the alveolar process and superomedially with the frontal process (see Image . Zygomatic Process of the Maxilla). The zygomatic process reinforces the structural integrity of the midface together with the alveolar process. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillary fractures are one of the most common emergencies presenting in the acute setting. [1] Due to the complex anatomy within this region and the proximity to vital structures, including the brain, early diagnosis, and precise treatment planning are of paramount importance. Numerous treatment methods are well-practiced globally, and these aim to restore the patient’s quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Pathological Lesions of The Jaws
retrieved_docs.content: Cone beam computed tomography may help evaluate large odontogenic and non-odontogenic cysts and benign tumors of the jaws. CBCT can show the extent of the lesion and the closeness to essential structures, such as the maxillary sinus; it may also help plan the surgical approach. [8] It is noteworthy that because CBCT provides very little information on soft tissues, it should not be implemented if there is a suspicion of malignancy. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: CT is the gold standard when examining bone for fractures. Plain films are appropriate if operating in an austere environment or there is restricted access to a CT scanner, but should not be considered first-line. MRIs are rare in a trauma setting and are not helpful when examining bony structures. A common pitfall with CT scans is not specifying the size of cuts. The finest possible cuts should be obtained, often 0.5 mm or 0.625 mm, in all views (axial, sagittal, coronal). The standard 2.5 mm cuts obtained from head CTs are much too big and will miss many fractures. A 3-dimensional reconstruction should also be obtained as often this will help elucidate fractures that might not be obvious when just examining slices from a single view.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Macrodontia often presents an aesthetic concern in the anterior maxilla, while macrodonts in the posterior region may contribute to crowding and impede normal tooth eruption. Radiographic monitoring is essential to assess potential eruption abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, medical computed tomography (CT) was used for 3D imaging in dental applications, but dental cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) quickly became the preferred option. This is because of CBCT's ability to produce volumetric images of the jaw bone at a reasonable cost and radiation dose, as well as its compact size, affordability, and ability to be located nearby or in-office. [2] It was first used to evaluate impacted teeth, apical lesions, and mandibular and maxillary diseases. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – Morphologically, the primary maxillary second molars (tooth A, J) resemble the permanent maxillary first molar. Rhomboidal in shape, the maxillary second molar has four cusps, two on both the buccal and lingual aspects. A fifth cusp can be present and is called the tubercle of Carabelli. Two well-defined cusps can be seen on the buccal view, and the lingual surface has three cusps: the mesiolingual, distolingual, and the cusp of Carabelli.

retrieved_docs.title: Mental Nerve Block -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mental foramen is most commonly located halfway between the alveolar crest and the inferior border of the mandible in direct line with the second premolar or between the two premolars [5] . However, it is hard to determine its exact position since it varies for every individual. On the horizontal plane, the foramen can localize mesial, distal, or between the apices of the premolar roots. On the vertical plane, it can be found above, at, or below the apices of the premolars. The variations are influenced by gender, age, race, and technique used for assessment (ie, palpation, Panorex, computed tomography scan). Furthermore, the alveolar bone resorption, typical of edentulous patients, makes the location of the mental foramen closer to the alveolar crest [6] . Despite its variability, multiple studies have demonstrated complete anesthesia when localizing the foramen with palpation and landmarks.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral canals can be present in different numbers in a certain tooth and cause problems during endodontic or root therapies. Radiopaque materials can be used to indicate the number and position of these canals.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Maxillary Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary sinus is a pyramidal air-filled cavity confined by the nasal cavity medially, the infraorbital rim superiorly, the alveolar process inferiorly, and the zygomatic process laterally. The floor of the maxillary sinus extends anteriorly to the premolar-canine region and posteriorly to the maxillary tuberosity, which can complicate extractions of impacted maxillary teeth. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Inferior Alveolar Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: The Rood classification is a valuable assessment tool that uses radiographs to predict IAN injury in 3rd molar extractions. [8] Several radiographic features on panoramic radiographs suggest proximity to the IAN, and the presence of 1 or more of these signs warrants a more complex imaging examination by CBCT. Signs to watch for include the loss of cortication of the inferior dental canal (IDC), seen as an interruption of the radiopaque line that lies above and below the IAN canal narrowing, alongside deviation of the IDC, darkening or narrowing of the roots, and dilaceration of the roots (see Image. Radiographic Features Suggestive of Close Proximity to the Inferior Dental Canal). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose Paranasal Sinuses -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary sinus is a pneumatic cavity in the maxillary bone (see Image. Right and Left Maxillary Sinuses, Panoramic Radiograph). [5] It is pyramidal, with a base looking to the nasal cavity, anterior, posterior, and superior walls, and a lateral blunt apex extending into the maxillary bone's zygomatic process. The size of the maxillary sinus at the adult stage is approximately 15 mL, [6] making it the largest paranasal sinus. The anterior wall corresponds to the facial surface of the maxillary bone, with 3 identified landmarks – the canine fossa and the infraorbital foramen and groove. The infraorbital foramen is located 5 to 8 mm below the midpoint of the inferior margin of the orbit. [7] The posterior wall correlates to the maxillary tuberosity, which shapes the anterior surface of the pterygopalatine fossa. [8] The posterior wall is closely related to the contents of the pterygopalatine fossa, including the pterygopalatine ganglion and several branches of the maxillary artery, vein, and nerve. [9] Tumors and infections of the maxillary sinus and oral cavity can extend to the pterygopalatine fossa and affect these essential structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: as a child modality, indicating that tomography (including CT) is considered a specialized subcategory within the spectrum of plain radiographic techniques. This network of hierarchical, mapping, and metadata relationships integrates various CT examinations—both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced, single‑region and multi‑region—under unified anatomical and procedural categories, supporting consistent terminology and facilitating comprehensive patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2366
retrieved_docs.content: Plain X-ray techniques encompass tomografie as a subordinate imaging method, indicating that tomografie is classified as a child technique within the broader category of plain X-ray imaging. This relationship highlights that tomografie derives from or is considered a specialized extension of standard plain X-ray procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is identified as a method_of, meaning it serves as a method of Computed tomography imaging - action, providing a structured approach for using CT scans in follow‑up assessments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291
retrieved_docs.content: Tomografie is recognized as a medical diagnostic imaging modality; it is classified within the broader category of plain X‑ray techniques and is considered a specific type of medical diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 853
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is classified as a Computed tomography imaging ‑ action and functions as a method of Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). This indicates that CT follow-up represents a specific imaging approach used when a standard listed CT procedure is not applicable, serving as a methodological option within broader diagnostic and interventional CT practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 159
retrieved_docs.content: stereotactic radiosurgery (الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية) is positioned as a specialized application within the overall framework of examination using radioactive isotopes. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the hierarchical and functional relationships that define nuclear medicine imaging, its diagnostic procedures, therapeutic applications, and site‑specific studies, all of which are essential for comprehensive patient evaluation and care.
